methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $30M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Captura is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Harry Atwater and CX Xiang, with $30M+ in total capital raised to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparative universe of 9 private peers and 1 public comparable (LNZA), with particular weight assigned to three same-stage, same-sector private companies: Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M).

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using total raised ($30M) as a proxy for current implied value, applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A-to-next-stage progression, yielding $60M. Peer-median analysis across the three identified comps produces a median valuation of $150M, with a range of $110M to $200M. The peer set reflects material heterogeneity in both stage and operational maturity within the Climate & Energy frontier-tech cohort.

Triangulation of these two methods supports a valuation range of $51M to $172M, with a median estimate of $105M. The wide dispersion ($51M–$172M) reflects meaningful uncertainty around where Captura sits relative to the peer cohort on dimensions including technology readiness, capital efficiency, and revenue traction. Stage-progression suggests conservatism relative to peer valuations, while peer-median signals higher market pricing for Series A climate-tech operators. Key uncertainties include Captura's technical differentiation relative to named peers and near-term capital requirements.
